@@ -140,6 +140,7 @@ {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-9y9x","title":"EPIC: User Discovery & Onboarding Enhancements","description":"# EPIC: User Discovery & Onboarding Enhancements ## Strategic Goal Make it effortless for users to discover what's installed, understand their environment, and continue learning—without requiring internet access or running heavy verification checks. ## Background & Rationale ### The Problem After ACFS installation, users face a discoverability gap: 1. **"What do I have?"** - No quick way to see installed tools, their aliases, or current state 2. **"What can I do?"** - 50+ aliases hidden in zshrc, not easily discoverable 3. **"How do I continue learning?"** - Onboard progress is basic, lessons are passive 4. **"I'm offline, now what?"** - Web wizard requires internet; no local reference ### Why This Matters - Beginners feel overwhelmed by unfamiliar environment - Power users forget aliases they don't use daily - SSH disconnections + reconnections lose context - The "flywheel" concept requires knowing what tools exist ### Current State Analysis - `acfs doctor`: Heavy (60+ checks), verification-focused, not quick-reference - `onboard`: Good lessons but passive, basic progress tracking - `acfs.zshrc`: Contains aliases but users don't read config files - Web wizard: Excellent but requires internet ### Proposed Solution Three complementary enhancements: 1. **`acfs info`** - Lightning-fast quick reference command - Shows: IP, hostname, uptime, installed tools, key aliases, onboard progress - Outputs: Terminal (default), JSON (--json), HTML (--html) - Design: Read state files only, NO verification/testing 2. **Enhanced Onboard Experience** - Progress visualization (percentage, progress bar) - "Try It" sections with expected output examples - Cheatsheet integration for command discovery - Completion celebrations 3. **Command Discovery System** - Categorized alias/command reference - Available via: `acfs help --commands`, `onboard --cheatsheet` - Extracted programmatically from zshrc (not duplicated) 4. **Static Dashboard Generation** (Optional Extension) - Generate HTML from `acfs info --html` - Serve on-demand with `acfs dashboard serve` - Works completely offline ## Success Criteria - [ ] User can see their full setup in <1 second with `acfs info` - [ ] User can discover all available aliases without reading zshrc - [ ] User can see onboard progress at a glance - [ ] User can access reference material offline - [ ] No duplication with existing `acfs doctor` functionality ## Non-Goals - Real-time monitoring dashboard (out of scope) - Persistent web server (security concerns) - Replacing the web wizard (it serves different purpose) ## Dependencies - None (enhances existing infrastructure) ## Downstream Impact - All feature beads in this epic depend on this for context - Implementation order matters (see dependency graph)","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-22T14:51:25.132198-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T15:01:03.690637-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T15:01:03.690637-05:00","close_reason":"EPIC complete: Strategy defined, success criteria documented. Downstream features (bags, d6my, gy0i) now unblocked for implementation."} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a1g","title":"Add Tailscale auth guidance to post-install summary","description":"## Task Update the post-install summary in `install.sh` to include Tailscale authentication guidance. ## Current State The installer prints a summary at the end with next steps. We need to add Tailscale auth. ## Implementation In `print_summary()` function, add: ```bash # Check if Tailscale needs auth if command -v tailscale &>/dev/null; then local ts_status ts_status=$(tailscale status --json 2>/dev/null | jq -r '.BackendState // "unknown"') if [[ "$ts_status" != "Running" ]]; then echo "" echo " 🔐 Tailscale (Secure Remote Access):" echo " sudo tailscale up" echo " → Log in with your Google account" echo " → Then access this VPS from anywhere!" echo "" else local ts_ip ts_ip=$(tailscale ip -4 2>/dev/null) echo "" echo " ✓ Tailscale connected: $ts_ip" echo "" fi fi ``` ## Ordering in Summary The post-install summary should guide users through auth in a logical order: 1. **Tailscale** (first - enables remote access) 2. **Claude Code** (primary coding agent) 3. **Codex CLI** (secondary agent) 4. **Gemini CLI** (uses native Google, likely already authenticated) 5. **GitHub** (if using git) ## Example Output ``` ============================================================ ACFS Installation Complete! ============================================================ Next Steps - Authenticate Your Services: 🔐 Tailscale (Secure Remote Access): sudo tailscale up → Log in with your Google account → Then access this VPS from anywhere! 🤖 Claude Code: claude → Log in with your Anthropic account (Google SSO available) 🤖 Codex CLI: codex login → Log in with your ChatGPT account (Google SSO available) 🤖 Gemini CLI: gemini → Log in with your Google account Pro tip: Use the same Google account for all services! ============================================================ ``` ## Considerations - Only show auth steps for services that need it - Show ✓ for services already authenticated - Keep it concise but actionable","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:58:34.656476-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T17:45:48.037286-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T17:45:48.037286-05:00","close_reason":"Tailscale auth guidance added to print_summary() in commit 4ee3080","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a1g","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bt5","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T17:03:15.487935-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a24x","title":"Fix brittle YAML parsing in security.sh","description":"","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-23T14:37:03.650348-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-23T15:07:55.611103-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-23T15:07:55.611103-05:00","close_reason":"Fixed YAML parsing to support arbitrary indentation"} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a7qh","title":"Add Playwright production smoke tests to CI workflow","description":"","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-25T12:17:52.900506-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-25T12:19:39.621137-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-25T12:19:39.621137-05:00","close_reason":"Added production-smoke.yml workflow that tests live site for JS errors after deploys, daily, and on-demand"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-a9nk","title":"Add 'Why VPS?' conceptual section before rent-vps","description":"# Task: Add 'Why VPS?' Conceptual Section ## Parent Epic [EPIC] VPS Conceptual Framework (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-19d4) ## Description Before asking users to rent a VPS, explain WHY they need one. Currently we jump straight to "pick a provider" without context. ## Implementation Details Location: apps/web/app/wizard/rent-vps/page.tsx Add a section at the top (or consider a new interstitial step): \`\`\`tsx A VPS (Virtual Private Server) is like having a computer that runs 24/7 in a datacenter. Here's why it matters for AI-assisted coding:
  • Always on: Your AI agents keep working even when your laptop is closed. Start a task, close your laptop, come back to results.
  • Persistent sessions: Your work continues exactly where you left off, even if your home internet drops.
  • Work from anywhere: Connect from your laptop, phone, or any computer. Same environment everywhere.
  • Separation: Keep AI coding separate from your personal computer. No battery drain, no cluttering your system.

Think of it as your "engine room" — the real work happens there, and your laptop is just the remote control.

\`\`\` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Appears before VPS provider selection - [ ] Explains benefits in user terms (not technical specs) - [ ] Uses the "engine room" metaphor from onboarding - [ ] Addresses "can't I just run this on my laptop?" implicitly - [ ] Works well on mobile ## UI/UX Notes - This should feel informative, not preachy - Benefits should be concrete and relatable - Keep it scannable with bullet points - Consider making it collapsible if users already understand VPS","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T13:40:51.789633-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:47.591527-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T14:02:47.591527-05:00","close_reason":"Already covered: rent-vps/page.tsx has extensive GuideExplain term='VPS' explaining why VPS is needed"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-aa1","title":"Implement atomic state file writes","description":"# Task: Implement atomic state file writes ## Context Part of EPIC: Phase-Granular Progress Persistence (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-5uu) ## Problem If the system crashes or SSH disconnects DURING a state write, the state file could be corrupted (partial JSON). This would cause the installer to fail on resume. ## Solution Use atomic write pattern: write to temp file, then mv (which is atomic on POSIX): ```bash save_state_atomic() { local state_file="$ACFS_HOME/state.json" local temp_file temp_file="$(mktemp)" # Write to temp file cat > "$temp_file" < current.json - name: Check for changes id: changes run: | # Parse JSON output changed=$(jq -r ".changed | length" current.json) echo "changed_count=$changed" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT if [[ "$changed" -gt 0 ]]; then echo "changed=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT jq -r ".changed[].name" current.json > changed_tools.txt else echo "changed=false" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT fi - name: Check stability (only if changes detected) if: steps.changes.outputs.changed == true id: stability run: | # Load previous run checksums from cache if [[ -f ".checksum-cache/previous.json" ]]; then prev_checksums=$(jq -r ".checksums" .checksum-cache/previous.json) curr_checksums=$(jq -r ".checksums" current.json) if [[ "$prev_checksums" == "$curr_checksums" ]]; then echo "stable=true" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT echo "Checksums stable across 2 runs - safe to PR" else echo "stable=false" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT echo "Checksums changed since last run - waiting for stability" fi else echo "stable=false" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT echo "First run with changes - caching for stability check" fi # Cache current state mkdir -p .checksum-cache cp current.json .checksum-cache/previous.json - name: Update checksums file if: steps.changes.outputs.changed == true && steps.stability.outputs.stable == true run: | ./scripts/lib/security.sh --update-checksums > checksums.yaml - name: Create Pull Request if: steps.changes.outputs.changed == true && steps.stability.outputs.stable == true uses: peter-evans/create-pull-request@v5 with: title: "chore(security): update upstream checksums" body: | ## Upstream Checksum Updates The following installer checksums have changed: $(cat changed_tools.txt | sed "s/^/- /") ### Stability Verified ✅ Checksums stable across 2 consecutive checks (12 hours apart) ### Action Required Please review the upstream changes before merging: $(cat changed_tools.txt | while read tool; do url=$(jq -r ".installers.$tool.url" checksums.yaml) echo "- [$tool]($url)" done) --- 🤖 Auto-generated by checksum monitoring workflow branch: auto/update-checksums labels: security, automated ``` ## Stability Check Logic ``` Run 1 (6 AM): Detects bun checksum changed → Cache new checksum → No PR yet Run 2 (6 PM): Same bun checksum as cached → Stable = true → Create PR Run 2 (6 PM): Different bun checksum than cached → Stable = false → Update cache → Wait for next run ``` ## Dependencies - Depends on: JSON output mode (agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ss3) - Creates: Auto-generated PRs ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Runs twice daily (12 hours apart) - [ ] Stability check requires 2 matching runs - [ ] Only creates PR when checksums are stable - [ ] PR body lists changed tools with links - [ ] Manual trigger available for testing","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:45:53.040595-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T14:55:17.637256-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T14:55:17.637256-05:00","close_reason":"GitHub Action workflow created for daily checksum monitoring with stability checking","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-coj","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-ss3","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T12:47:37.794807-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-csv","title":"Wire acfs-update into install.sh and PATH","description":"## What Integrate acfs-update into the ACFS installation process: 1. Copy acfs-update script to ~/.acfs/bin/ 2. Ensure ~/.acfs/bin is in PATH (already done via acfs.zshrc) 3. Create 'acfs' wrapper script that supports subcommands (acfs update, acfs doctor, etc.) ## Technical Details - Script lives at ~/.acfs/bin/acfs-update - May want 'acfs' wrapper: acfs update -> acfs-update, acfs doctor -> acfs-doctor - install.sh copies script during finalize phase - Script should be executable (chmod +x) ## Considerations - Should we have acfs-update standalone or as 'acfs update' subcommand? - Subcommand approach is more elegant but requires wrapper script - For v1, standalone acfs-update is simpler ## Decision v1: Standalone acfs-update script v2: Add 'acfs' wrapper with subcommands ## Success Criteria - [ ] acfs-update installed to ~/.acfs/bin/ - [ ] Executable and in PATH after install - [ ] Works immediately after fresh install","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:08.593655-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T15:22:28.551425-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T15:22:28.551425-05:00","close_reason":"Wired acfs-update into install.sh finalize phase with proper permissions and PATH linking","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-csv","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-u5z","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:34.053226-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-csv","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3mp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:34.225526-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-csv","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-db0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:34.394293-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-csv","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-hby","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:34.559188-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-csv","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-obi","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:34.725276-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-csv","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-3ht","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:34.897036-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-csv","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-pom","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:27:35.061635-05:00","created_by":"daemon"},{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-csv","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-d0g","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T13:32:19.749545-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} discovered."} +{"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-cvwu","title":"Investigate wizard pages 503 errors in production","description":"","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2025-12-25T12:17:53.919583-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-25T12:17:53.919583-05:00"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-cxk","title":"Feature: Lesson Content Pages","description":"# Lesson Content Pages ## Parent Epic Webapp Learning Hub (Part 2) - x04 ## Scope Create the 9 lesson pages that render the onboard lesson content in the webapp. Content source is the markdown files in `acfs/onboard/lessons/`. ## The 9 Lessons | # | Slug | Title | File | Duration | |---|------|-------|------|----------| | 0 | welcome | Welcome & Overview | 00_welcome.md | 5 min | | 1 | linux-basics | Linux Navigation | 01_linux_basics.md | 8 min | | 2 | ssh-basics | SSH & Persistence | 02_ssh_basics.md | 6 min | | 3 | tmux-basics | tmux Basics | 03_tmux_basics.md | 7 min | | 4 | agent-commands | Agent Commands | 04_agents_login.md | 10 min | | 5 | ntm-core | NTM Command Center | 05_ntm_core.md | 8 min | | 6 | ntm-palette | NTM Prompt Palette | 06_ntm_command_palette.md | 6 min | | 7 | flywheel-loop | The Flywheel Loop | 07_flywheel_loop.md | 10 min | | 8 | keeping-updated | Keeping Updated | 08_keeping_updated.md | 4 min | ## Content Strategy ### Reuse vs. Enhance The markdown files are the source of truth. We can: 1. **Direct render** - Load and render as-is (simple, consistent) 2. **Enhance for web** - Add interactive elements, images, videos (better UX) 3. **Adapt** - Rewrite for web consumption (most work, divergence risk) Recommendation: Start with direct render, enhance incrementally. ### Web-Specific Additions For each lesson, consider adding: - Hero image or illustration - Estimated reading time - "Try it" interactive prompts - Related lessons sidebar - External links (official docs) ## Implementation Approach Use `getStaticParams` to generate static pages at build time: ```typescript // app/learn/[slug]/page.tsx export async function generateStaticParams() { return LESSONS.map(lesson => ({ slug: lesson.slug })); } export default async function LessonPage({ params }) { const lesson = getLessonBySlug(params.slug); const content = await loadMDX(`acfs/onboard/lessons/${lesson.file}`); return ; } ``` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] All 9 lessons accessible at `/learn/[slug]` - [ ] Content renders correctly with proper styling - [ ] Code blocks have syntax highlighting - [ ] "Mark Complete" button works - [ ] Previous/Next navigation works - [ ] Mobile-responsive ## Dependencies - Requires: Learning Hub Core Infrastructure (tor) ## Blocked By - tor (Learning Hub Core Infrastructure) ## Blocks - Wizard Integration (needs lessons to exist for cross-links)","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2025-12-21T18:17:30.584798-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T18:25:51.220475-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T18:25:51.220475-05:00","close_reason":"All 9 lesson pages implemented in tor commit (3c7f940): routes at /learn/[slug], markdown rendering with syntax highlighting, progress tracking, mobile-responsive layout, prev/next navigation"} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-cxr","title":"[ubu.11] Implement error recovery and graceful degradation","description":"# Implement Error Recovery and Graceful Degradation ## Purpose Handle upgrade failures gracefully and provide recovery paths. This is CRITICAL - a failed upgrade can leave the system in an unusable state. ## Failure Modes and Recovery ### 1. Network Failure During Package Download **Detection**: apt-get returns error 100 or timeout **Recovery**: ```bash ubuntu_retry_with_backoff() { local cmd="$1" local max_retries=5 local delay=30 for i in $(seq 1 $max_retries); do if eval "$cmd"; then return 0 fi log_warn "Attempt $i failed, retrying in ${delay}s..." sleep $delay delay=$((delay * 2)) # Exponential backoff done return 1 } ``` ### 2. Disk Full During Upgrade **Detection**: apt-get returns ENOSPC error **Recovery**: ```bash ubuntu_emergency_cleanup() { log_warn "Attempting emergency disk cleanup..." # Clear apt cache apt-get clean # Remove old kernels (keep current + previous) apt-get autoremove -y # Clear journal logs over 100MB journalctl --vacuum-size=100M # Report remaining space df -h / | tail -1 } ``` ### 3. Interrupted dpkg **Detection**: `/var/lib/dpkg/lock-frontend` exists, dpkg --configure fails **Recovery**: ```bash ubuntu_fix_dpkg() { log_warn "Fixing interrupted dpkg..." # Wait for any running apt while fuser /var/lib/dpkg/lock-frontend >/dev/null 2>&1; do sleep 5 done # Configure any unpacked packages dpkg --configure -a # Fix broken dependencies apt-get -f install -y } ``` ### 4. do-release-upgrade Failure **Detection**: do-release-upgrade returns non-zero **Recovery**: ```bash ubuntu_recover_upgrade() { local error_code="$1" case "$error_code" in 1) # General failure - try fixing deps apt-get -f install -y apt-get dist-upgrade -y ;; *) log_error "do-release-upgrade failed with code $error_code" log_info "Manual intervention may be required" ;; esac } ``` ### 5. Reboot Fails / System Does Not Come Back **Detection**: Cannot detect from same system **Mitigation**: - State file preserves progress - User can SSH back and check status - Resume service will restart upgrade **User Recovery**: ``` If system fails to boot: 1. Boot from recovery mode (hold SHIFT during boot) 2. Select "root shell" 3. Run: dpkg --configure -a && apt-get -f install 4. Reboot normally ``` ## Graceful Degradation If upgrade cannot proceed, allow ACFS to continue on current version: ```bash ubuntu_upgrade_with_fallback() { if \! ubuntu_do_upgrade; then log_error "Ubuntu upgrade failed" # Check if system is still functional if apt-get update &>/dev/null; then log_warn "System is functional. Continuing ACFS on current Ubuntu version." log_warn "Some features may not work optimally." # Mark upgrade as skipped, not failed state_upgrade_skip "upgrade_failed_graceful_degradation" # Continue with ACFS installation return 0 else log_error "System may be in inconsistent state. Manual recovery needed." return 1 fi fi } ``` ## Error Logging All errors should be logged with context: ```bash ubuntu_log_error() { local component="$1" local message="$2" local timestamp=$(date -Iseconds) echo "[$timestamp] ERROR [$component] $message" >> /var/log/acfs/upgrade.log # Also save to state for resume debugging state_upgrade_set_error "$component: $message" } ``` ## Post-Failure Diagnostics Create diagnostic dump on failure: ```bash ubuntu_create_diagnostic_dump() { local dump_file="/var/log/acfs/upgrade_diagnostic_$(date +%Y%m%d_%H%M%S).txt" { echo "=== ACFS Upgrade Diagnostic Dump ===" echo "Timestamp: $(date)" echo "" echo "=== Ubuntu Version ===" cat /etc/os-release echo "" echo "=== Disk Space ===" df -h echo "" echo "=== Memory ===" free -h echo "" echo "=== dpkg Status ===" dpkg --audit echo "" echo "=== APT History (last 50 lines) ===" tail -50 /var/log/apt/history.log echo "" echo "=== ACFS State ===" cat /var/lib/acfs/upgrade_state.json 2>/dev/null || echo "No state file" echo "" echo "=== Last 100 lines of upgrade log ===" tail -100 /var/log/acfs/upgrade.log 2>/dev/null || echo "No upgrade log" } > "$dump_file" log_info "Diagnostic dump saved to: $dump_file" } ``` ## Priority: P1 (Critical) This task is P1 because error handling is what separates a robust tool from a fragile script. Users will encounter failures - the question is whether they can recover.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:25:49.431838-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-21T16:42:00.726784-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-21T16:42:00.726784-05:00","close_reason":"Added error recovery functions: ubuntu_retry_with_backoff, ubuntu_emergency_cleanup, ubuntu_fix_dpkg, ubuntu_recover_failed_upgrade, ubuntu_create_diagnostic_dump, ubuntu_upgrade_with_fallback for graceful degradation.","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-cxr","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-0ge","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-21T16:26:42.710018-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]} {"id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-cxz3","title":"TASK: Design acfs info output format and data model","description":"# TASK: Design acfs info output format and data model ## Context First task in implementing `acfs info`. Need to define exactly what data is shown and how it's structured before implementing. ## Deliverables ### 1. Data Model (Shell Variables/Struct) Define what information is gathered: ```bash # System Info INFO_HOSTNAME="" INFO_IP="" INFO_UPTIME="" INFO_OS_VERSION="" INFO_OS_CODENAME="" # Installation State INFO_TOOLS_INSTALLED=() # Array of installed tool IDs INFO_TOOLS_SKIPPED=() # Array of skipped tool IDs INFO_TOOLS_TOTAL=0 INFO_INSTALL_DATE="" # Onboard Progress INFO_LESSONS_COMPLETED=() INFO_LESSONS_TOTAL=9 INFO_NEXT_LESSON="" # Quick Commands (top 8) INFO_QUICK_COMMANDS=( "cc:Launch Claude Code" "ntm new X:Create session" # ... ) ``` ### 2. Output Format Specification Terminal output with sections: - System (hostname, IP, uptime, OS) - Installed Tools (summary counts, categories) - Quick Commands (top 8 aliases) - Onboard Progress (bar, percentage, next lesson) - Footer (links to doctor, cheatsheet) ### 3. JSON Schema For `--json` output: ```json { "system": { "hostname": "...", "ip": "...", "uptime": "...", "os": {"version": "...", "codename": "..."} }, "installation": { "date": "...", "tools_installed": [...], "tools_skipped": [...], "total_tools": 52 }, "onboard": { "lessons_completed": [...], "total_lessons": 9, "next_lesson": "..." }, "quick_commands": [...] } ``` ### 4. HTML Template Skeleton Basic structure for `--html` output. ## Data Sources Document where each piece of data comes from: - Hostname: `/etc/hostname` or `hostname` - IP: `hostname -I | awk '{print $1}'` - Uptime: `uptime -p` - OS: `/etc/os-release` - Install state: `~/.acfs/state.json` - Onboard: `~/.acfs/onboard_progress.json` ## Acceptance Criteria - [ ] Data model documented - [ ] Terminal output format mocked up - [ ] JSON schema defined - [ ] HTML template skeleton created - [ ] Data sources mapped ## Notes This is design-only. Implementation comes in subsequent tasks.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-22T14:55:47.589344-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-22T15:07:45.632779-05:00","closed_at":"2025-12-22T15:07:45.632779-05:00","close_reason":"Complete implementation: info.sh with terminal, JSON, HTML, minimal output formats; integrated into acfs CLI","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-cxz3","depends_on_id":"agentic_coding_flywheel_setup-bags","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-22T14:55:58.176868-05:00","created_by":"jemanuel"}]}
